sentences.text: David John Landau, L-A-N-D-A-U.
Yes, I did have during the resistance-- Jan [INAUDIBLE]
1920, the 1st-- the 15th of March.
Warsaw, Poland.
The memories have begun to outweigh the plans, and there is nothing abnormal in that.
What is out of the norm is the cage of the past in which I am locked in for the rest of my life.
The cage does not exist outside my mind, but it is as real to me as if it were made of steel rods, cast in concrete, and fenced off with barbed wire.
If, by some chance in the natural laws, I were to live for 1,000 years, I would still remain caged in that invisible jail for the full 1,000 years.
I am not the only one trapped in such a cage.
I belong to a breed of people which I have never-- which have never existed before my generation and will be extinct from the human race within the next 20 to 30 years.
All of our breed suffer from the same apparition.
The breed is called survivors, because we cheated the huge Nazi killing machine of death camps and ghettos and came out alive from places where we were meant not to survive.
We live in a time warp from which only death will release us, and each of us carries within the mind an imagery cage which dominates our lives.
Those who have become my friends or who have known me after I passed the age of 25 consider fate that always smiles on me.
This is not the whole truth.
They don't know my younger years when fate and I were locked together for years in one cage.
And we lived in an endless fight, a knife fight-- fate against me and I against fate.
Not I, but fate gave in.
Instead of making me its victim, it became friendly to me.
And I became a survivor.
Only then, it became my friend.
But the imagery-- imaginary, beg your pardon-- cage has remained.
The steel bars have been covered over the normal life in a free country.
But it is still a cage.
I welcome you as a visitor to relive with me the years in which fate and I fought the battles of life and death with unsheathed knives.
You will not be able to learn it from the first-hand after my generation will die out.
The formative years-- three large cities found their special place in the history of the Second World War.
They suffered the cruelest form of torture at a time when torture was the norm.
Starvation was endemic and cruelty.
Deprivation and death visited tens of millions of people.
Stalingrad was the first of them.
Its name became a synonym of bravery and death, of the fateful, final decision of the world.

At Stalingrad, the human will to live overcame the power of total evil on its march to enslave the world for 1,000 years with its plan to wipe out whole races of human species, beginning with the Jews.
Berline was the last of the three.
From that city, the evil beast began its murderous conquering march.
And in Berlin, it was finally put to death.' That's it.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] Mother, Miriam.
Father, Yitzak.
Brother, Eliazur.
Brother, Metek.
Brother, Juziek.
And sister, Sylvia.
We're middle class.
We'd been-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
We'd been engaged in tanneries and in building trade.
It was a home not religious.
We been Jews, having friends, Jews and Christians but knowing that we're Jewish quite well.
Mother's side was all-- including my grandfather-- socialist, more to the left.
Father was not involved with socialism, but in his business and his home and children.
I lived in a very comfortable, very comfortable life.
We did have a very comfortable life-- parents which never screamed at us, never put their hand on us.
Our punishment was done in a different way, where if we did want something and we did something which wasn't right, that was refused to give us something or to help us in the way.
That was the punishment.
Nothing else-- no screaming, [? nothing. ?]
Can you tell us from your brothers and sisters, how you interacted with them, played with them, spoke to them?
We been all three years apart.
When I was, for example, 12, my older brother was 15, and the oldest, 18.
The younger brother was nine and sister, six.
That was always, that every three years, a child-- five children.
Did you-- you went to school?
Yes, I went.
I went to school, to private school and then went to a gymnasium.
I haven't been very good at school.
And at the age of about 15, I left school and have a private tutor in my home.
I always been looking to be together with father in the business.
I never did want to go to school and be happy at school.
So we decided that the best way would be that I'll have a tutor home and help father in the business, which I did.
Pardon?
Mother's completely not religious, mother's family, including my grandfather.
No.
No.
Pardon?
Not specially.
We used to go out to some events, sporting events, but no.
We, we kept-- everyone kept for himself.
Because three years difference, and the age was quite a bit at the age of 12, 15, 18.
So we kept to ourselves.
From 1933, I would say, we've seen a little bit changes with our good friend Adolf being on another side in Germany.
Yes, we did found some little incidents here and there.
At universities, I heard from mine friends and brothers that something is happening.
And--
Such as excesses like beating up of the Jewish students and things like that.
But that was in a small form at that very time.
By other students, yes-- by the national organization, the students belonging to the national organization, which did have a little, a little medal in their--
Lapel which was called [POLISH SPEECH].
Pardon?
That's a sword of one of the kings.

That wasn't a very happy-- in football matches-- or soccer which is called here-- soccer matches, when it was playing Jewish club with a Christian club, it was calls from the public which wasn't very sympathetic for Jews.
We seen a little, little change in it.
You know, I been, I been always going.
If I went up sometimes, I went myself to the [? cabana ?] for skiing.
Because I was a skier from the age of six.
I said that, you know, from the age of six, I been a skier.
And sometimes, father and mother came with me to the [? cabana, ?] which was about 400 kilometers from Warsaw.
400 Kilometers approximately.
From Warsaw.
I think that was about 400 kilometers.
To ski?
I been skiing till 1938.
Everything-- the condition changed from year to year, yes, in mine, mine observation, yes.
Because I did have a lot of Christian friends.
And I noticed it was a change, yes.
Being inclined to socialism, I could see that I prefer practically to split it, like, to be separate from the Jewish socialists.
And in some cases, I discovered that very left socialism can be quite anti-Semitic.
This mean that I did have to wait till 1953 where Khruschev said plainly that as many boys will be at university, that many boys I like to see-- Jewish boys I like to see in the mines.
So I did have to wait till 1953 to be convinced what I thought in 1938, '39, what I've been thinking of it, that that was right.
Mine, mine, assumption, assumption-- whatever you call it-- was right at that time.
That can exist.
We did see slowly different attitude towards the Jews.
Because the propaganda of Adolf came deep and deeper to Poland.
Oh, my mother was a very peaceful being, left-- She was a very peaceful woman.
And I don't know.
I haven't heard anything between them.
She only accept what I did, that I was to side with mother.
And she was very happy with it.
And at one stage, as a boy of 16 and a half, I went-- I tried to go to Spain.
I went as far as [?
Lyon. ?] And I was turned back because that was signed-- the night was signed the Pact of Nonaggression.
And every boy which was under the military age or not in the army was sent back to Czechoslovakia.
The ones who had been over had been smuggled back to Spain.
That was the border of France and Spain.
France and Spain.
Thank you.
Basque country.
What sporting--
No.
I been the representative of Warsaw ski team in 1938.
And that was the only thing I knew.
Of?
Yes.
I been around, yes, yes.
[INAUDIBLE].
This is the socialist organization from the young, young ones-- the, the, the young generation, like 15, 16-year-olds.
No.
Mixed.
That was the socialist organization.
The Germans?
The Germans marched into Poland very early.
Yeah.
That's the first day, the first day.
Because our tannery start to slow down.
Everything start to--
One time, [INAUDIBLE] the first thing was to the radio.
We'd been told that.
And having in the tannery-- already, I been at that time at the tannery-- We knew immediately.
And then we did have a phone call from our mother, which was very distressed.
And we knew that the war start.
1St of September.
Pardon?
The year was 1939.
The tannery was affected because we lost few workers which went to the army and immediately called up forthwith.
And we did have immediately the secret police there, because we did-- in our place, not knowing-- we did, we did--
Employ, employ one of the top people, which when the Germans came into Warsaw, we discovered.
Ernest or something, [?
Franken. ?] He was then a [INAUDIBLE] for, for fur and skins in Poland.
He was a high ranking SS officer.
And he was working with us for about 15 years in the tannery.
Nobody knew about it, that he was--
A German spy.
Yeah.
And then immediately when the Germans came in to Warsaw about two, three weeks later, he came to our place asking my father to start off the tannery again, which stopped.
Because we did have a sign on the tannery, whoever will come into the tannery is death penalty.
The tannery was closed.
Death penalty for coming into the tannery, as soon as the German came.
Because we been working for the military.
The Polish military.
So they closed the factory and put a sign.
Whoever will pass through the door or open it up is death penalty.
That was the first.
They didn't only on our factory.
They did on every one which was a factory working for the army or for--
Pardon?
If I'm not mistaken, [?
Franken ?] for sure-- but Ernest or, or something like that.
Oh, that was for-- would be for, would be for the army.
But we didn't open it.
My father found any excuse not to open.
He brought to us a full cart, a, a, a wagon or whatever you like to call it, with food.
Was enough to feed the whole our flats, all the people, for about four weeks.
We opened a kitchen from the food which we'd been given from him.
No, in the apartment house, but downstairs.
In the, in the, in the yard.
We opened a kitchen.
And we fed all the place, because it was very hard to get food at that time.
Oh, yes.
He knew that we are Jewish.
Yeah, because he did that in the beginning.
They wouldn't mind to open any factory which could work for the Germans, factories which have been working for the government, for the army, for things like that.
Yes, they would open gladly any one at that time.
So he know-- he knew us.
And he was in a very high position.
So he came in his uniform, beautifully dressed.
And he did want to convince my father to start up.
But my father found every excuse not to open it.
And how did he handle the excuses?
Pardon?
He wasn't happy with it.
But he took it nice.
Because after all, he was working with us for 15 or so years.
So he was a little bit, at that time, nicely towards my father.
And he said to him that he's not happy and that he prepared to have it start off.
But if father doesn't want to, it's bad, bad luck [INAUDIBLE].
Yes.
Right aw-- that was a shortage immediately.
When the Germans walked into Warsaw, start immediately queues for bread.
And yes, start immediately.
Pardon?
People did stock.
That's why they made the shops empty.
And the people who did have shops start to hide their little bit of food and sell it slowly out for better prices.
Yes, that's, that's nothing unusual in, in, in time of war, that something like that happen.
Because after mine experience-- 75 years, I seen it-- that had happen in every country.
If there's a war or starting a war, the food is the first thing to be hit.
Because that's the only thing which you can get some more money during wars than you get before the war [INAUDIBLE].
I haven't been with the family all the three weeks.
Because after few days, I been-- my brothers had been taken to the army, the older brothers, both of them.
One was in the officer school before the war.
And one was just a university student which was taken immediately to the army.
Both of them.
The Polish army.
And before the war-- in the start, in the start of the war.
They have been taken to the army, both of them.
And mother was with me, with mine sister and younger brother that was the what left in, at home-- father, mother, and three children.
Now, I been told by father that he spoke to a big ranking officer which was in charge with the, the buyer, the military buyer of the-- of our goods for the army.
And he told him that he better see me out of Warsaw, the oldest, which is left with-- in the family, David, which was 19-year-old.
Better if he will leave Warsaw.
If I will, David.
And go towards, towards Lublin.
Because we're going to have a stand there against the Germans.
We're going to re-army, re-organize, And we're going to have a stand near Lublin.
So better if a young person will walk to-- will go towards Lublin.
And that was a lot of young people you could see at the-- on the road, very, very-- quite a lot when I went out about five, six days after, after the, the war start when the Germans.
That was already-- the Germans have been, I think, already in Warsaw, or next to Warsaw.
And they start to march out from the-- from Warsaw towards Lublin.
Marched out-- went out with groups of people which were going out of Warsaw.
They'd been leaving Warsaw.
No, no, no.
Not at all.
But on the way, we tried to get into, to places like over the road.
We went actually towards Kowel.
And on the road, I met few people which I knew, Christian, Jewish people.
And then I met up with one boy which was from a village.
And we been marching together, together.
And few little things happened on, on the way because--
Not as a Jew, but normally the attacking of the, of the planes, the roads, the German planes.
Then we caught two spies.
Two spies.
Yeah.
Dressed in, in clerical, clerical [INAUDIBLE].
And they been walking in the back of the, of the column and giving signals in the nighttime.
Because we been mostly walking in the night.
In the day, we used to hide in some of the forest or something which you could hide the whole groups.
Hundreds of people who marched-- and army, too, lot of the army, all in trucks or in train or just walking.
So they caught them and they-- they caught them seeing that they're giving signals in the nighttime.
Because always in the morning, we're being attacked by planes.
You were strafed by planes.
Immediately in the morning-- 5:00, 6:00 in the morning.
Was a little bit light, immediately the planes came in very low over us and attacking with everything, even with bundles of grenades.
With bundles of grenades from the top, from the airplane.
Oh, that took me, I don't know.
It's hard to remember now-- quite a few days.
We been marching about 30 to 40 kilometers a day.
Once we got there, I got to Kowel.
I been there.
Then I went to Vilna and then back from Vilna to Kowel and back to Bialystock and home.
Because I been told that my mother is very, very ill by some people which came later, friends of mine.
And I'd been told [? that dying. ?] I went to a place which was called [INAUDIBLE] and Bartosze in Poland, not far from the border, which was then the border of the Russians with the Germans.
I found there some people which I been told about in Warsaw if I will pass through there to see them.
And so the people start to think of a little organization, of a little partisan organization.
But unfortunately, didn't work long.
Didn't work long because we didn't-- we haven't been helped by an organization.
We didn't ask any to be helped.
But we did want to form ourselves.
And instead of guns, we did have pieces of sticks which we knocked at the windows and telling the peasants that we are the a group of million people.
And we want some food.
This was the first thing [INAUDIBLE].
We been given the first two, three days.
And then they chased us out of the village.
They said if we come again-- most probably, they seen that this is a stick, not a gun.
We decide to stop it and to go back home, not to play partisan without an organization.
For the?
For the?
No.
That was about-- no, I can't remember.
I been friendly right practically to the war with those two places.
And I did have there friends.
No, I can't remember names.
No.
And after the war, I tried to go there.
And I been there to both places when I been and start to look for something very wrong.
Because I used to say to the people when I've been looking for it-- first of all, my pronunciation wasn't exactly as it should be.
Because when you say, for example, Bartosze, a peasant will say Bar-to-sher.
It is changing the-- because that's a peasant.
Yeah.
Normally, that's Bartosze and [INAUDIBLE].
Now, I went there.
And I made one big mistake when I went after the war to have a look at it.
During the war, everything was all right.
I knew where I'm going.
But after the war, 19-- that was much later.
Because I went out of Poland in 1946, I think-- '45, end '45.
And when I went there, it was about 1958, 1960 the first time-- 1958.
And I been told by the-- I told the people that's, that's a very important thing.
I said, he did have a child of about a year, a boy.
And I made a mistake because the child could be already 12-year-old or 15-year-old.
Or whatever it is in 1958.
But I made a mistake.
And I couldn't find the people.
Pardon?
When?
When I--
After the war?
I told myself knowing the people there and being with them and trying to do something, that I been in Warsaw in my place during the war.
So I told myself, I'll have a look.
And I'll see what is happening to them.
